comments:
Summary:
The paper proposes an LSTM-based encoder with two decoders (reconstruction and classification) to perform semi-supervised learning on motion capture (MOCAP) sequences. The main contributions are (1) combining reconstructive and discriminative decoders in a single recurrent encoder-decoder framework for semi-supervised training, (2) empirical exploration of architecture variants and hyper-parameters, and (3) defining a more realistic actor-based test split on the HDM05 dataset and demonstrating benefits from adding unlabeled CMU data.

Strengths:
- The paper addresses a realistic and useful issue: how to leverage abundant unlabeled MOCAP data to improve supervised action classification. The idea of a multi-decoder recurrent encoder is intuitive and well motivated.
- The authors identify an important evaluation issue (random shuffling producing overly optimistic results) and propose an actor-held-out test split that better reflects generalization to new actors.
- Reasonable engineering choices and practical details are provided (preprocessing, minibatch handling, initialization, learning rate schedule, gradient clipping), and experiments show that adding unlabeled CMU data reduces train-test gap and improves accuracy.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- Quantitative reporting in the provided text is incomplete. The manuscript references tables and figures (e.g. Table 1, Table 3, Figure 4) but concrete numerical results and comparisons are not shown here; therefore it is hard to assess the magnitude of improvement or compare fairly to strong baselines.
- Comparisons to prior work are limited. While the authors reimplemented Cho & Chen and discuss other references, more rigorous comparisons to recent sequence models (including Srivastava et al. and other RNN/LSTM-based classifiers) and to supervised-only strong baselines would strengthen the claims.
- Statistical rigor is lacking: experiments appear to be single runs with the same initialization in some ablation tests, and no variance/error bars or significance tests are reported. Semi-supervised gains should be demonstrated across multiple random seeds and reported with confidence intervals.
- Some design choices require further justification: the choice of classifier hidden size (2048), the procedure to set the classification/reconstruction weight α, and why the reconstruction decoder must be initialized with encoder cell state rather than using a summary vector — these are discussed but would benefit from more thorough ablation.
- Generality beyond HDM05/CMU is unclear. The approach may be specific to this domain; experiments on other sequence datasets or a clearer discussion of applicability would help.
- Presentation has repetitions, and parts of the provided text include duplicated sentences and formatting artifacts; clarity could be improved in several sections.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Include the missing tables/figures and provide full numeric results: absolute accuracies, confusion matrices, and improvement magnitudes when adding unlabeled data. Report standard deviations across multiple runs.
- Add stronger baselines and recent related methods (e.g. recent supervised RNNs, other semi-supervised sequence approaches). Provide an ablation study showing the effect of each architectural choice and a sweep over α.
- Provide more detail on preprocessing and how variable-length sequences, padding and masking are handled during training and loss computation.
- Release code and the proposed actor-held-out split to facilitate reproducibility and adoption.
- Clean up duplicated text and polish presentation for clarity.

Conclusion:
The paper proposes a sensible and practically useful extension of encoder-decoder recurrent models to semi-supervised MOCAP classification and emphasizes an important evaluation protocol (actor-held-out split). However, the empirical evidence presented in the provided text is incomplete and would need fuller quantitative comparisons, statistical reporting, and ablations to be fully convincing. With those improvements the work would be of interest to the community.
